```{=html}
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Source+Sans+Pro:wght@400;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
```

## Overview

In this tutorial, we will cover the basics of R needed for all other
tutorials. Once done with this tutorial, you should understand
**Objects**, **Functions**, and **Operators**. Having a firm
understanding of these concepts are necessary building blocks for more
advanced work in R.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
flowchart LR
    A((Objects)) --> B((Functions)) --> C((Operators))
```

### Commands used in this tutorial

1.  `<-` — assignment
2.  `c()` — concatenate
3.  `data.frame()` — create dataframes
4.  `#` — comments
5.  `class()` — check object class
6.  `$` — access columns
7.  `str()` — object structure

## Objects {.section-highlight}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
flowchart LR
    A((Objects)) --> B((Functions)) --> C((Operators))
    style A fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#1a1a1a,color:#fff
```

The Objects section covers four sub-topics. Work through each tab in order.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Creating Objects

R is an object oriented language. You create "objects" and can apply
functions to those objects.

The most basic thing you need to learn is about the assignment operator
`<-`. This is how you create objects. The way this looks in code is the
following:

`object_name <- value`

So lets pretend we wanted to make an object named `x` with a
value of `4`.

We would enter the following code `x <- 4`. Give it a try and run the
following code `x <- 4`, then enter `x` and R should return `4`.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example
:::

```{webr-r}
x <- 4
x
```

After running this, `x` is now an object whose value is 4.

::: {.quiz data-id="q1" data-type="single"}
**For the R code `game <- 7`, what is the name of the object?**

- [ ] `<-`
- [x] game
- [ ] 7
:::

::: {.quiz data-id="q2" data-type="single"}
**What is the value of `y` from the following code?** `x <- 3`, `z <- 10`, `y <- x + 2`

- [ ] 10
- [x] 5
- [ ] 15
- [ ] 2
:::

Sometimes you don't want to create an object, but will want to make a
comment in your code. If you put `#` in front of text, R will not
process it. This allows you to make comments in your code.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create an object named `uoidaho` with a value of `100`
:::

```{webr-r}
# Write your code here

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
uoidaho <- 100
```
</details>

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create an object called `rulez` with the value of 5
:::

```{webr-r}
# Write your code here

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
rulez <- 5
```
</details>

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create `agreed` by adding `uoidaho` and `rulez`
:::

```{webr-r}
uoidaho <- 100
rulez <- 5

# Add them together and store in agreed

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
uoidaho <- 100
rulez <- 5

agreed <- uoidaho + rulez
```
</details>

::: {.quiz data-id="q3" data-type="single"}
**What is the value of the object `agreed` from the above example?**

- [ ] 100
- [ ] 5
- [x] 105
:::


### Object Names

R and computers in general are very good at following orders, the
problem is that you need to be very specific or you might get an error
message. R is very sensitive. When naming objects, use the following
conventions:

1.  No spaces
2.  Lower case (R is sensitive to capitalization)
3.  Use `_` instead of `.`
4.  Use descriptive names
5.  Avoid special characters like `!@&`
6.  Don't start with a number (they can end with a number, but they can't
    start with one)

Take the following quiz and identify the problem with the following
object names.

::: {.quiz data-id="q4" data-type="multi"}
**What is wrong with the following object name? Click all that apply:** `University of Idaho <- 100`

- [x] Has spaces
- [x] Mix of lower and upper case
- [ ] Use `_` instead of `.`
- [ ] Generic name
- [ ] Uses special character and/or starts with a number
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — fix the name `University of Idaho <- 100`
:::

```{webr-r}
# Fix the object name and assign value 100

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
university_of_idaho <- 100
```
</details>

::: {.quiz data-id="q5" data-type="single"}
**What is wrong with the following object name?** `10idaho! <- 100`

- [ ] Has spaces
- [ ] Mix of lower and upper case
- [ ] Use `_` instead of `.`
- [ ] Generic name
- [x] Uses special character and/or starts with a number
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — fix the name `10idaho! <- 100`
:::

```{webr-r}
# Fix the object name and assign value 100

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
idaho <- 100
```
</details>

::: {.quiz data-id="q6" data-type="single"}
**What is wrong with the following object name?** `university.of.idaho <- 100`

- [ ] Has spaces
- [ ] Mix of lower and upper case
- [x] Use `_` instead of `.`
- [ ] Generic name
- [ ] Uses special character and/or starts with a number
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — fix the name `university.of.idaho <- 100`
:::

```{webr-r}
# Fix the object name and assign value 100

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
university_of_idaho <- 100
```
</details>


### Object Structures

Objects come in many forms. In the previous section, we were making
objects that represented a single ***atomistic*** value. However,
objects can have complex structures such as vectors, matrix, array,
dataframe, or list.

For the purpose of simplicity we will focus on vectors and dataframes.
Unless you spend a lot of time programming, iterating, or looping, you
will spend the majority of time working with dataframes and to a lesser
extent, vectors.

A vector takes a series of atomistic values to form an object.

`object_name <- vector`

A useful command is the concatenate command `c()`. This allows you to
combine different elements separated by `,`.

For example, `object <- c("value_1","value_2","value_3")`

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create a vector named `alphabet` with values a, b, c, d
:::

```{webr-r}
# Create a vector named alphabet

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")
```
</details>

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create a vector named `pie` with values 3, 14, 15, 926
:::

```{webr-r}
# Create a vector named pie

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
```
</details>

Vectors can be made with numbers, strings in `""` (i.e.,
characters/words), objects, or a combination of the two.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — create a vector named `pie_not_number` with values 3, x, apple, and 7
:::

```{webr-r}
# Create a vector named pie_not_number

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
```
</details>

#### Dataframe

Lets make a dataframe. Dataframes can be thought of as connected vectors
with the same number of items.

Dataframes are composed of columns and rows and are the object structure
used to analyze data. Columns represent variables, and rows represent
observations in a dataframe. You can create a dataframe with the command
`data.frame()` using the following structure:

```r
dataframe_name <- data.frame(
  column_name_1 = vector_1,
  column_name_2 = vector_2,
  ...
)
```

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — build a dataframe and check its class
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")

first_df <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphabet,
  pie_num = pie,
  random_vec = pie_not_number
)

class(first_df)
```

::: {.quiz data-id="q7" data-type="single"}
**For the value `x <- 86`, object `x` has what object structure?**

- [x] Atomistic single value
- [ ] Vector
- [ ] Dataframe
:::

::: {.quiz data-id="q8" data-type="single"}
**For the value `x <- c(86,100,2)`, object `x` has what object structure?**

- [ ] Atomistic single value
- [x] Vector
- [ ] Dataframe
:::


### Object Classes

All objects have a *class* in R. This tells R how to handle the object.
In dataframes, the data within a column needs a shared class, but
different columns can have different classes. You can identify the class
of an object with the argument/function `class()`.

`class(object_name)`

For example, `class(first_df)` returns the value "data.frame" indicating
that the object's class is a data.frame.

What is important is that when working in a dataframe, you will want to make
sure the class of each of your columns (i.e., variables) is correct. To
access a column from a data frame you need to use `$` in the following
way:

`dataframe_name$column_name`

Using the `$` operator, lets see what class the `alpha` column is from
your `first_df` dataframe: `class(first_df$alpha)`

It should return "character". This type of class is how strings
are stored. The column named `pie_num` is a numeric class. Numbers are
stored as either numeric (can include decimals) or integer (whole
numbers only).

The main take away is that objects have a class and that you can
determine what the class of an object is.

`str()` is a useful command and one that I use more than any
other command in R. It shows the class, number of observations, variables,
and the names and classes of each column.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Your turn — try `class()` and `str()` on `first_df`
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")
alpha_is_vowel <- c(TRUE,FALSE,FALSE,FALSE)

first_df <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphabet,
  pie_num = pie,
  random_vec = pie_not_number,
  vowel = alpha_is_vowel
)

# Try class() and str() on first_df

```

<details><summary>Show Solution</summary>
```r
class(first_df)
str(first_df)
```
</details>

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — check the class of each variable using `$`
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")
alpha_is_vowel <- c(TRUE,FALSE,FALSE,FALSE)

first_df <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphabet,
  pie_num = pie,
  random_vec = pie_not_number,
  vowel = alpha_is_vowel
)

class(first_df$alpha)
class(first_df$pie_num)
class(first_df$random_vec)
class(first_df$vowel)
```

::: {.quiz data-id="q10" data-type="single"}
**Referencing `first_df`, what class is the variable `alpha`?**

- [ ] Double
- [ ] Integer
- [x] Character
- [ ] Logical
:::

::: {.quiz data-id="q11" data-type="single"}
**Referencing `first_df`, what class is the variable `vowel`?**

- [ ] Double
- [ ] Integer
- [ ] Character
- [x] Logical
:::

:::

## Functions {.section-highlight}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
flowchart LR
    A((Objects)) --> B((Functions)) --> C((Operators))
    style B fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#1a1a1a,color:#fff
```

Now that you understand the nature of objects, it is important to
understand functions — the other building block of programming in R.

The function performs some operation on an object. For example,
`class()` is a function that returns an object's class.

Functions can be broken down into this structure:

`new_object_name <- function(argument)`

Arguments can be: 1. commands to feed the function, 2. objects, 3. other
functions, 4. operators.

In the case of `class(first_df)`, the argument is a dataframe object. In
the case of the function `mean()`, which calculates the average of a
vector/column/variable, you need to specify the column you want it to
run the function on.

`mean(first_df)` returns an error. `mean(first_df$pie_num)` returns the
mean of the column `pie_num`.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — using `mean()` on a dataframe column
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")
alpha_is_vowel <- c(TRUE,FALSE,FALSE,FALSE)

first_df <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphabet,
  pie_num = pie,
  random_vec = pie_not_number,
  vowel = alpha_is_vowel
)

mean(first_df$pie_num)
```

Now you can store the output of a function as an object, for example:
`avg_pie_num <- mean(first_df$pie_num)`

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — storing function output as an object
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)
alphabet <- c("a","b","c","d")
alpha_is_vowel <- c(TRUE,FALSE,FALSE,FALSE)

first_df <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphabet,
  pie_num = pie,
  random_vec = pie_not_number,
  vowel = alpha_is_vowel
)

avg_pie_num <- mean(first_df$pie_num)
avg_pie_num
```

Creating objects through functions is essentially what you do in R.
Everything else is really just various levels of increasingly
sophisticated functions to accomplish similar objectives.


## Operators {.section-highlight}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
flowchart LR
    A((Objects)) --> B((Functions)) --> C((Operators))
    style C fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#1a1a1a,color:#fff
```

While R can be boiled down to objects and functions, it also does a good
job handling mathematical calculations and logic.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Math Operators

The following commands are the basic arithmetic operators used in R.

| Math           | Operator |
|----------------|----------|
| Addition       | `+`      |
| Subtraction    | `-`      |
| Multiplication | `*`      |
| Division       | `/`      |
| Exponent       | `^`      |

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Try it yourself — experiment with each math operator
:::

```{webr-r}
x <- 56
y <- 914

x + y
x - y
x * y
x / y
x^y
```

### Logic Operators

Often, you will want to create an object with the help of some logical
operators. The following are the logical operators used in R:

| Logic                         | Operator |
|-------------------------------|----------|
| Less Than                     | `<`      |
| Greater Than                  | `>`      |
| Less Than or Equal to         | `<=`     |
| Greater Than or Equal to      | `>=`     |
| Equal to                      | `==`     |
| *Not* Equal to                | `!=`     |
| Or                            | `\|`     |
| And                           | `&`      |

For example, `logical_test <- pie == pie_not_number`

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — logical comparisons return TRUE or FALSE
:::

```{webr-r}
pie_not_number <- c(3,"x","apple", 7)
pie <- c(3,14,15,926)

logical_test <- pie == pie_not_number
class(logical_test)
logical_test
```

This returns a new class of `logical` that is either TRUE or FALSE.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Run this example — try `and`, `or`, and `not` operators
:::

```{webr-r}
another_test <- 3 == 2+1

x <- 4
y <- 3

and_statement <- x & y < 4
or_statement <- x | y < 3
not_statement <- x != y

another_test
and_statement
or_statement
not_statement
```

:::


## Conclusion

The core of R is creating objects using functions and operators. Before
anything more advanced it is important to have a good understanding of
objects, functions, and operators. You will get plenty of practice using
these concepts in the other tutorials and in class. When you are ready,
start working on the knowledge check.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5
flowchart LR
    A((Objects)) --> B((Functions)) --> C((Operators))
```

## Knowledge Check

The knowledge checks are required for each class. If you have already
completed them, then submit the knowledge check code to your instructor.
If you have not completed it, submit your response to your instructor.
Wrong answers are not counted against you, but you will not receive a
code until the correct knowledge check responses have been provided.
